perturbation.original_text: 1.7 shall not, and shall procure that any associated company of yours shall not, employ, seek to employ, or solicit any pe rson employed by us or the Business or who holds or otherwise has access to Confid ential Information either at the date of this Agreement or at any time during negotiations for the sale of the Business or during the period of twelve months from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 This agreement shall expire two years from the date of execution. 1.7 shall not, and shall procure that any associated company of yours shall not, employ, seek to employ, or solicit any pe rson employed by us or the Business or who holds or otherwise has access to Confid ential Information either at the date of this Agreement or at any time during negotiations for the sale of the Business or during the period of twelve months from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the non-solicitation clause (1.7) after the agreement's expiration clause (2.5) obscures its enforceability, implying it only applies within the two-year agreement term. This creates ambiguity about whether the non-solicitation agreement extends beyond the general confidentiality agreement's expiration.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8(a)(1) of the NLRA prohibits employers from interfering with, restraining, or coercing employees in the exercise of their rights to self-organization, to form, join, or assist labor organizations. A non-solicitation clause that is ambiguous, vaguely worded, or overly broad can be interpreted as an attempt to restrict employee's rights to associate freely with each other, even after a business sale falls through. By placing the clause after the agreement's expiration date, it implies the non-solicitation agreement is limited to the life of the broader confidentiality agreement which is two years. This arrangement reduces transparency concerning how long employees are subject to these constraints, which can discourage employees from joining labor organizations or engaging in any group activity. The placement of the non-solicitation clause can make the scope seem limited to the two-year period, and thus more easily overlooked.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw that creates a legal contradiction. The original contract clearly defines the duration of the non-solicitation clause as extending twelve months from the agreement date. By moving this clause after the agreement's expiration clause, the changed text creates ambiguity regarding its enforceability beyond the two-year agreement term. This ambiguity weakens the enforceability of the non-solicitation clause, potentially interfering with employees' rights to associate freely, as it is vaguely worded, which is not in line with Section 8(a)(1) of the NLRA, and could be interpreted as an attempt to restrict employee's rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the National Labor Relations Act, specifically section 8, indicating its relevance.

perturbation.original_text: 1.  UNDERTAKING In consideration of our Agreement to supply to you certain written or oral information relating to the Business and entering into discussions relating to the sale of the Business to you (howsoever structured), you the Recipient hereby undertake to us, the Vendor for our benefit and that of the Busine ss, except to the extent that we have given prior written consent, whether or not negoti ations for the sale of the Business proceed, that you:
perturbation.changed_text: 2.  ACKNOWLEDGEMENT AND CONFIRMATION You acknowledge and confirm as follows: 1.  UNDERTAKING In consideration of our Agreement to supply to you certain written or oral information relating to the Business and entering into discussions relating to the sale of the Business to you (howsoever structured), you the Recipient hereby undertake to us, the Vendor for our benefit and that of the Busine ss, except to the extent that we have given prior written consent, whether or not negoti ations for the sale of the Business proceed, that you:
perturbation.explanation: By placing the UNDERTAKING clause under the ACKNOWLEDGEMENT clause, we undermine its importance. The "UNDERTAKING" is the central promise made by the Recipient; placing it under an acknowledgment lessens its legal significance. The Recipient might understand their contractual responsibilities less clearly.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 20 deals with the concept of misunderstanding. By placing the main obligations (UNDERTAKING) under the heading of Acknowledgement, it creates a situation where a party could genuinely misunderstand the scope and intention of the agreement. An 'acknowledgement' seems to be a statement of fact rather than a commitment to future action and responsibility. This arrangement implies that these sections contain affirmations of already-known facts rather than new promises being made. A party can claim that due to this placement, they were confused about what exactly they were agreeing to. This potential confusion directly relates to 20(1)(a) - which describes situations where both parties are in disagreement because of their misunderstanding. In these situations, the contract isn't binding as the parties never agreed to the same terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Restatement (Second) of Contracts - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Restatement of Contracts ) Widely cited American legal treatise The Restatement (Second) of the Law of Contracts is a legal treatise from the second series of the Restatements of the Law , and seeks to inform judges and lawyers about general principles of contract common law. It is one of the best-recognized and frequently cited legal treatises [ 1 ] in all of American jurisprudence . Every first-year law student in the United States is exposed to it, and it is a frequently cited non-binding authority in all of U.S. common law in the areas of contracts and commercial transactions. [ 2 ] The American Law Institute began work on the second edition in 1962 and completed it in 1979; the version in use at present has a copyright year of 1981. [ 3 ] For an explanation of the purpose of a restatement of law, see Restatement of the Law . Use and commentary [ edit ] Legal scholars and jurists have commented extensively on the Restatement, both in contrasting it with aspects of the first Restatement, and in evaluating its influence and effectiveness in reaching its stated objectives. It is in this context of direct review that one can find numerous arguments both favoring and criticizing some aspects of the Restatement as an independent source of legal scholarship. [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] Although several sections of the Restatement contained new rules which sometimes contradicted existing law, courts citing these sections have predominantly adopted the Restatement's view, citing them as a court would cite statute or code. [ 9 ] Far more common, however, is the practice of citing the Restatement to clarify generally accepted doctrine in every major area of contract and commercial law. It is in this context of legal research that one can find the Restatement used as direct substantiation and persuasive authority , to validate the arguments and interpretations of individual legal practitioners. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] Legacy [ edit ] Although the Restatement of Contracts is still an influential academic work, certain aspects have been superseded in everyday legal practice by the Uniform Commercial Code . Specifically, the UCC has replaced the Restatement (Second) of Contracts in regard to the sale of goods. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts remains the unofficial authority for aspects of contract law which find their genesis in the common law principles of the United States and, previously, England. perturbation.justification: The change introduces a structural flaw that undermines the clarity and enforceability of the 'UNDERTAKING' clause. The Law Explanation argues that placing the 'UNDERTAKING' under 'ACKNOWLEDGEMENT' could lead a party to misunderstand the scope of the agreement. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this placement weakens the legal significance of the undertaking, making it less clear that the Recipient is making a binding commitment. While the scraped snippets do not directly address this specific issue, the general principles of contract law suggest that clear and unambiguous placement of obligations is important. By diminishing the prominence of the 'UNDERTAKING,' the modified text creates a structural flaw that could make the contract more susceptible to legal challenges based on misunderstanding, as mentioned in the Law Explanation referring to Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 20.
